turns.content: I'm trying to implement a phased computation in my Rust library but I keep thinking thread::JoinHandle won't work because it's not a barrier. Why does this even work this way when we need all workers to finish before the next phase starts?

That is a very common confusion for juniors because JoinHandles are primarily designed for task ownership and cancellation rather than mutual waiting. Think of thread::Barrier like a traffic light at an intersection where every lane must stop until everyone else has cleared before proceeding to the next phase, whereas JoinHandles are more like sending away packages on a conveyor belt without stopping the line.

I tried using it but I'm getting an error saying no threads have reached the barrier yet. Can't you just use a Mutex or Channel instead of this? Why is thread::Barrier necessary for my startup's data pipeline?

You are right that Mutex and Channels solve different problems like shared state or message passing, but Barrier is specifically designed to make N threads wait until all N have arrived at a checkpoint before any can continue. If you use a Mutex instead of thread::Barrier for your pipeline, the threads might start reading data before everyone has finished writing their phase which causes race conditions and data corruption.
Okay I see but why does it feel like we are building a dependency graph when using this? Isn't that just adding overhead to my startup's performance?

It is indeed an extra cost compared to simple blocking, but the safety guarantee of thread::Barrier prevents you from having to manually coordinate who finishes before who starts which would be error-prone and complex to manage. If you skip this synchronization step in your pipeline, you will likely encounter deadlocks or inconsistent state that is much harder to debug than the small overhead of waiting.
